coredata.dc:title: CDs-g-C3N4-oleaginous yeast hybrid system: Microbial lipid synthesis and fermentation residual reutilization
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Yadong
Wang, Shanshan
Lv, Shaopeng
Wang, Laiyou
Guo, Shuxian
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of solar energy and fast-growing heterotrophic microbes for biofuel production has been recognized as a promising approach to achieve carbon neutrality and address energy crisis. In this work, we synthesized different kinds of photocatalysts based on graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4). We found that carbon dots modified-graphitic carbon nitride (CDs-g-C3N4) showed the highest photocatalytic activity. Subsequently, we developed a photocatalyst-microbe hybrid (PMH) system by combining CDs-g-C3N4 with an oleaginous yeast strain, Cutaneotrichosporon dermatis ZZ-46. Under visible light irradiation, the lipid yield of this PMH system reached 1.70 g/L at 120 h, representing a 36 % increase compared to the control. The photocatalytic reaction-induced ROS and the reductive photogenerated electrons facilitated ZZ-46 cells to synthesize more lipids. Furthermore, the fermentation residual of this PMH system was reutilized to prepare biochar via pyrolysis. The biochar generated at 550 °C (BC-550) demonstrated exceptional adsorption capabilities, particularly with a 57 % adsorption rate for methylene blue (MB), and maintained its perfect adsorption efficacy even after five regeneration cycles. These results offer promising avenues for addressing energy shortages and environmental contamination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biofuel
Oleaginous yeast
Photocatalyst-microbe hybrid
Microbial lipid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4, CDs-g-C3N4
PMH system construction and lipid content analysis
NADH regeneration, NADP+/NADPH ratio and ROS generation analysis
Preparation and characterization of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
Determination of the adsorption capacity of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
Adsorption kinetics of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
Determination of the reusability of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
Construction and optimization of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system
Lipid synthesis mechanism of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system
Preparation and characterization of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
Adsorption capacity evaluation of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
Kinetic dynamics and reusability of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open-end Funds of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment
Henan Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbial Resources and Fermentation Technology, Nanyang Institute of Technology
Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province and Talent
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22378196), Open-end Funds of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment (SH20231208), Open Project of Henan Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbial Resources and Fermentation Technology, Nanyang Institute of Technology (HIMFT20210206), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province and Talent research start-up fund of Nanjing Tech University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4.

A-B: XRD patterns (A) and FI-TR spectra (B) of pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4, CDs-g-C3N4; C: Adsorption of MB onto pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4 in darkness; D: Degradation of MB caused by pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4 under illumination; E: First-order kinetic plots of the degradation of MB caused by pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4; F: The rate constant of MB degradation caused by pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4.

A: Impacts of CDs-g-C3N4 concentration on the degradation of MB under irradiation; B: Effect of light and CDs-g-C3N4 on the growth of ZZ-46 cells; C: Effects of TEOA on the growth of ZZ-46 cells; D-F: SEM images of CDs-g-C3N4 (D), ZZ-46 cells (E) and CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system (F).

A: Effects of CDs-g-C3N4 concentration on the lipid yield (containing 1 g/L TEOA, operated for 120 h); B: Effect of TEOA concentration on the lipid yield (containing 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4, operated for 120 h); C: Changes of the PMH system lipid yield under different conditions during 120 h; D: Lipid yield of PMH system under different conditions at 120 h.

A: Content of NADH after 6 h reaction; B: Changes of NADH content at the presence of CDs-g-C3N4 and TEOA; C: Effect of CDs-g-C3N4 on NADPH/NADP+ ratio in ZZ-46 cells under irradiation; D: Effect of CDs-g-C3N4 on ROS level in ZZ-46 cells under irradiation (* and ** indicated p < 0.05 and p < 0.01, respectively).

Preparation and characterization of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar. (A: Preparation route; B: SEM image of ZZ-46 cells (scale bar = 20 μm); C: SEM image of the lyophilized CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH residual (scale bar = 20 μm); D: SEM image of BC-550 (scale bar = 20 μm); E: SEM image of BC-550 (scale bar = 1 μm).

A-B: Adsorption performances of biochar pyrolyzed under temperatures towards MB (A: Change of MB concentration MB for 1 h; B: Comparisons of MB adsorption rate after 1 h); C: Influence of solution temperature on the adsorption of MB; D: Effect of Initial pH on the adsorption of MB (**** indicated p < 0.001).
A: First-order and second-order kinetics plots for the adsorption of MB by BC-550; B: Reusability of BC-550.

head.ce:title: CDs-g-C3N4-oleaginous yeast hybrid system: Microbial lipid synthesis and fermentation residual reutilization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biotechnology and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211800, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biotechnology and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biotechnology and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211800, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbial Resources and Fermentation Technology, Nanyang Institute of Technology, Nanyang 473004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbial Resources and Fermentation Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbial Resources and Fermentation Technology, Nanyang Institute of Technology, Nanyang 473004, People'’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Biotechnology and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211800, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biotechnology and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of solar energy and fast-growing heterotrophic microbes for biofuel production has been recognized as a promising approach to achieve carbon neutrality and address energy crisis. In this work, we synthesized different kinds of photocatalysts based on graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4). We found that carbon dots modified-graphitic carbon nitride (CDs-g-C3N4) showed the highest photocatalytic activity. Subsequently, we developed a photocatalyst-microbe hybrid (PMH) system by combining CDs-g-C3N4 with an oleaginous yeast strain, Cutaneotrichosporon dermatis ZZ-46. Under visible light irradiation, the lipid yield of this PMH system reached 1.70 g/L at 120 h, representing a 36 % increase compared to the control. The photocatalytic reaction-induced ROS and the reductive photogenerated electrons facilitated ZZ-46 cells to synthesize more lipids. Furthermore, the fermentation residual of this PMH system was reutilized to prepare biochar via pyrolysis. The biochar generated at 550 °C (BC-550) demonstrated exceptional adsorption capabilities, particularly with a 57 % adsorption rate for methylene blue (MB), and maintained its perfect adsorption efficacy even after five regeneration cycles. These results offer promising avenues for addressing energy shortages and environmental contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The non-renewable nature of fossil fuels and the environmental problems they cause compel us to develop renewable energy (Chu et al., 2017). Biodiesel, a high-performance biofuel, offers a viable alternative to fossil diesel. Plant-extracted oils, such as rapeseed oil and palm oil, can be used to produce biodiesel. However, the majority of these oils are utilized in the food and chemical industries, limiting their availability for biofuel production on a large scale (Mishra and Goswami, 2018). Oleaginous microorganisms can synthesize and store oils exceeding 20 % of their dry weight. When compared to animals and plants, oleaginous microorganisms offer numerous advantages in oil production, such as high efficiency, reduced reliance on climatic conditions, and the ease of continuous large-scale production. Using oleaginous microorganisms to synthesize oil and produce biodiesel is an effective solution to address the challenges mentioned above.

Oil is a kind of highly reductive substance, and the biosynthesis of oil requires a lot of reducing power, NADPH. Oleaginous microorganisms predominantly acquire NADPH through the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP). Yet, PPP competes with central carbon metabolic pathways for glucose and releases CO2, which leads to the consumption of metabolic flux and the decrease of the lipid yield (Zhao et al., 2015). Therefore, it is of great significance to find additional ways to supply NADPH for oleaginous microorganisms (Wang et al., 2017b). Some autotrophic microorganisms, such as oleaginous microalgae, produce NADPH in the photoreaction phase under sunlight exposure. They subsequently utilize this NADPH to assimilate CO2 during the dark reaction phase for oil synthesis. These microalgae produce NADPH while reduce the consumption of organic matters and the production of CO2. Yet, the maximum lipid synthesis efficiency of these oleaginous microalgae is only ~5 g/m2/d, which cannot meet the commercial requirement (30 g/m2/d) (Chen et al., 2018). In this context, scientists endeavor to seek a solution for the following question: “Is it possible to endow the fast-growing heterotrophic microbes with the capacity of light-harvesting and NADPH production similar to microalgae?”

Fortunately, the recently reported photocatalyst-microbe hybrid (PMH) system, seems to provide a new solution for the aforementioned question. When the photocatalysts (made of semiconductor materials) are bombarded by photons whose energy is equal to or even higher than that of band-gaps, photocatalytic reactions occur on the surface of photocatalysts and generate hole-electron pairs. The positively charged holes (h+) are eradicated by the supplemented sacrificial electron donors (SED), allowing numerous photogenerated electrons to be transferred into the microorganisms adjacent to the photocatalysts. Then a large amount of reducing power-NADPH is generated to drive metabolic pathways and synthesize valuable chemicals (Fu et al., 2018; Nichols et al., 2015; Sakimoto et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018). The PMH system innovatively harnesses light to supply microbes with reducing power, enabling the production of valuable chemicals. Considering the operation of PMH system and the lipid synthesis pathways in oleaginous yeasts, we believe that integrating oleaginous yeasts with photocatalysts in a PMH system might be a viable method for lipid production. However, we have not found any relevant report so far. In addition, when the target chemicals are extracted, the residual biomass of the PMH system needs to be treated since it contains photocatalysts and may cause unexpected environmental risks. However, there is no report about the treatment and reutilization of the residual biomass of PMH system.

g-C3N4 is a typical metal-free semiconductor with a defined crystalline laminar structure. There are two basic units in g-C3N4, the 3-s-triazine ring and the triazine ring (Xu et al., 2018). The planar amino groups of g-C3N4 connect the 3-s-triazine units, which leads to a wide light-response spectrum up to 460 nm. g-C3N4 can be easily tuned to form various morphologies, since the adjacent layers are held together by the weak van der Waals forces (Zhang and Jiang, 2022). The catalytic activity of g-C3N4 can be upgraded by many methods, such as morphology redesign, metal loading and coupling with other functional materials (Zhang et al., 2023a; Zhang et al., 2024a; Zhang et al., 2023b; Zhang et al., 2023c; Zhang et al., 2024b; Zhang et al., 2022). Among these methods, using carbon dots (CDs) as a co-catalyst to modify g-C3N4 has garnered many attentions because 1) CDs can serve as effective electron-transfer materials; 2) CDs interact with g-C3N4 strongly and provide numerous active sites due to their high oxygen content and sp2 character of CC bonds; 3) the preparation of CDs is convenient and low-cost (Wang et al., 2017a; Yuan et al., 2023). These interesting properties make g-C3N4 a popular star in the photocatalytic field (Hatamie et al., 2018). Meanwhile, we previously screened out an oleaginous yeast strain, Cutaneotrichosporon dermatis ZZ-46, which exhibited a satisfying lipid accumulation capacity (Wang et al., 2020a). And it has been found that extracellular electrons can be easily delivered into C. dermatis by the electron transport pathways on its cell membrane (Lagarde and Jaffrezic-Renault, 2011).

In this work, we synthesized a photocatalyst based on g-C3N4, which was then combined with C. dermatis ZZ-46 to establish a PMH system. We optimized the operation conditions of this PMH system and investigated its lipid synthesis mechanism. Moreover, the residual biomass of this PMH system was converted into biochar through pyrolysis. The resultant biochar demonstrated exceptional adsorption capabilities and recyclability, indicating its significant potential for environmental remediation applications. Our work provides a new route for microbial lipid synthesis and the reuse of PMH system residual biomass. These advancements are of great significance for producing biofuels based on solar energy and heterotrophic microbes and addressing environmental pollution challenges in the future.

Melamine, FeCl3, zinc acetate, MnSO4, l-ascorbic acid and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4, CDs-g-C3N4

ce:list-item.ce:para: Preparation of g-C3N4: Melamine was heated to 550 °C using a SK2–2-12TPA2 vacuum tube furnace (Zhengzhou Honglang Instrument Equipment Company, China) at a rate of 1 °C/min, and then held constant for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting powder were cooled down to room temperature and then they were collected and ground to obtain the g-C3N4 powder.

Preparation of Fe-g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2020b): 25 mL 12 g/L g-C3N4 suspension was sonicated for 2 h, after which FeCl3 was added and reached a final concentration of 10.56 g/L. The resulting mixture was stirred for 2 h and it was placed in a microwave oven for irradiation (1 min, 400 W). Then the mixture was taken out and cooled down in cold water for 3 min. This irradiation-cool procedure was repeated for 10 times. Finally, the resultants were filtered and rinsed three times with distilled water and ethanol. Finally, the resultants were collected by centrifugation.

Preparation of ZnO-g-C3N4 (Kong et al., 2017): g-C3N4 powder (1 g/L) and zinc acetate (1 g/L) were mixed together in 20 mL ethanol. The mixture was continuously stirred for 20 min and then it was kept at 80 °C water bath for 5 h. Afterwards, the resulting mixture was collected and dried to obtain ZnO-g-C3N4.

Preparation of Mn-g-C3N4: 0.5 g g-C3N4 and 1.2 g MnSO4 were mixed together in 60 mL deionized water and stirred for 2 h. Then the suspension was dried at 110 °C and the resulting sample was heated to 300 °C using a SK2–2-12TPA2 vacuum tube furnace (Zhengzhou Honglang Instrument Equipment Company, China) at a rate of 5 °C/min, and then held constant for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere.

Preparation of CDs-g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2017a): 0.6 g l-ascorbic acid was thoroughly dissolved in a mixture containing 30 mL deionized water and 30 mL ethanol. 0.6 g g-C3N4 was dispersed into the aforementioned solution by stirring for 1 h and sonicating for 30 min. Afterwards, the resultant mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave, heated to 180 °C and held constant for 4 h. Then it was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the resulting precipitates were collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 30 min) and washed three times with ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the samples were dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:para: A PCX50C multi-channel photochemical reaction system (Beijing Perfect-Light Technology Company, China) equipped with a 5 W LED light source and a 420 nm filter was used to conduct the photocatalytic experiment. Photocatalyst (final concentration 1 g/L) was added into 50 mL 10 mg/L MB solution and kept stirring in darkness for 1 h to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the photocatalytic degradation experiment was initiated. To determine the residual MB concentration, 1 mL solution was sampled every 1 h and filtered (0.22 μm). The absorbance of the solution was measured using a UV1200 ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mapada Instrument company, China) at a wavelength of 664 nm.

C. dermatis ZZ-46 strain was from Nanyang Center of Industrial Culture Collection (Nanyang, China). ZZ-46 strain was inoculated and cultured in YEPD liquid medium (20 g/L glucose, 10 g/L yeast extract and peptone 10 g/L). All cultivations were carried out at 28 °C under constant orbital shaking at 150 rpm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PMH system construction and lipid content analysis
ce:section.ce:para: ZZ-46 strain (10 % inoculation) was inoculated into 20 mL nitrogen-limited medium, which was composed of 20 g/L glucose, 0.25 g/L yeast extract, 1 g/L (NH4)2SO4, 0.5 g/L KH2PO4, and 0.25 g/L MgSO4·7H2O. 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4 was added into the culture broth to construct the CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system. The PMH system was operated in darkness or under illumination (32.8 mW/cm2) for 120 h. To analyze the lipid content, 1 mL fermentation broth was sampled every 24 h. The cells were harvested by centrifugation and resuspended in 1 mL distilled water. Then the cell solution was mixed with 2 mL sulfuric acid and reacted in a water bath at 90 °C for 20 min. Afterwards the resulting mixture was cooled down to room temperature and added with 3 mL 0.25 g/L vanillin phosphate and reacted at room temperature for another 20 min. Finally, the absorbance of the resultant mixture was measured by a UV1200 ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mapada Instrument company, China) at a wavelength of 526 nm.

A scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SU8020, Hitachi company, Japan) was used to observe the morphologies of photocatalysts, ZZ-46 cells, CDs-g-C3N4/ZZ-46 hybrids and biochar. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) (TF-G20, Thermo Fisher Scientific Company, USA) was used to observe the morphologies of pristine g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4. The crystal composition of photocatalysts was analyzed using a Smartlab X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Company, Japan). The functional groups of the materials were determined using a Nicolet iS5 FI-TR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Company, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NADH regeneration, NADP+/NADPH ratio and ROS generation analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate NADH regeneration in the photocatalytic reaction system, a mixture containing 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4, 100 μL [Cp*Rh(bpy)H2O]2+ (Cp*: pentamethylcyclopentadienyl, bpy: 2, 2 ‘-bipyridyl), 200 μL NAD+, 100 μL 20 % TEOA, 100 μL PBS buffer (pH = 9) and 500 μL ultra-pure water were prepared. The absorption curve of this mixture between 280 nm to 500 nm under irritation were detected using a UV1200 ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mapada Instrument company, China). The amount of generated NADH was determined by the intensity of the absorption peak of NADH at 340 nm. For comparison, the control group (no CDs-g-C3N4 and no TEOA) and the group with 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4 but no TEOA were set and detected using the same procedures.

ZZ-46 cells in culture medium (~109 cells/mL) was co-cultured with 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4 (or 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4 + 2 g/L TEOA, or 2 g/L TEOA) for 36 h under illumination. Then the cells were harvested by centrifugation. To determine the NADP+/NADPH ratio and ROS content, a NADP+/NADPH assay kit (Shanghai Beyotime Biotechnology Company, China), a ROS assay kit (Nanjing Cullen Biotechnology Company, China) and an ELIASA microplate reader (5119500C, Thermo Fisher, USA) were utilized. ZZ-46 cells cultured in darkness with no photocatalyst were used as the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: After being operated for 120 h, the mixture containing CDs-g-C3N4 and ZZ-46 cells in the PMH system were collected by centrifugation. The mixture was sonicated and the lipid was removed. Then the residual biomass of PMH system (denoted as the PMH residual) was dried and pyrolyzed at 350 °C, 450 °C and 550 °C, respectively, for 2 h under N2 atmosphere using a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The resulting biochar was washed with distilled water and dried. Biochar prepared under different temperatures was designated as BC-350, BC-450 and BC-550.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the adsorption capacity of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: 0.05 g BC-350 (or BC-450, or BC-550) were dispersed in 50 mL 10 mg/L MB solution, which was then kept stirring. 1 mL of the reaction solution was sampled every 10 min and filtered (0.22 μm). Then the absorbance of the solution was determined by a UV1200 ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mapada Instrument company, China) at a wavelength of 664 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption kinetics of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: Biochar was added into 30 mL 10 mg/L MB solution to reach a final concentration of 1 g/L. Then the solution was stirred for 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 and 120 min, respectively, and filtered (0.22 μm). The absorbance of the solution was determined by a UV1200 ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mapada Instrument company, China) at a wavelength of 664 nm. Pseudo-first order and Pseudo-second order kinetic models were calculated by a previously reported method (Choi et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the reusability of PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: Ethanol was employed to regenerate the used adsorbent, PMH system residual biomass derived biochar. Then the regenerated adsorbents were used to conduct the adsorption tests by the same method described in Section 2.9. The adsorbents were reused and regenerated for five cycles and the adsorption capacity changes were recorded (Wang et al., 2022).

The significance of differences was analyzed by Origin 8.0 software (OriginLab, USA). Differences with p < 0.05 were considered statistically significant. All experiments were done at least for three times.

We synthesized the yellow pristine g-C3N4 powder using melamine as the precursor. Then Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4 were synthesized based on pristine g-C3N4 (Fig. S1). SEM images indicated that the pristine g-C3N4 possessed irregular layered structures. Fe doping did not significantly change the microstructure of g-C3N4. In contrast, there were a large number of ZnO particles scattered on the surface of ZnO-g-C3N4, which increased the reaction sites and improved the photocatalytic activity. Although Mn doping did not substantially change the g-C3N4 morphology, it introduced more surface defects on Mn-g-C3N4, which could boost the photocatalytic activity. The morphology of CDs-g-C3N4 was similar to the pristine g-C3N4, indicating that the addition of l-ascorbic acid and the carbonization process did not damage the structure of g-C3N4 (Fig. 1 ).

The crystal composition and phase information of pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4 were characterized using XRD. The pristine g-C3N4 had two characteristic peaks around 13.1° and 27.3° (Fig. 2A). The peak at 13.1° corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of g-C3N4. This was attributed to the tri-s-triazine repeating structural units in the plane (Du et al., 2020). The peak at 27.3° corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of g-C3N4, which was attributed to the interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic rings in g-C3N4 (Lu et al., 2017). These two peaks still existed in the samples of Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4, which indicated that the incorporation of metal ions or carbon dots (CDs) did not compromise the fundamental structure of g-C3N4. However, compared with pristine g-C3N4, the intensity of the peak near 27.3° slightly weakened in Fe-g-C3N4. This result was possibly due to the Fe ions adhering to the layered structure of g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2020b). More peaks appeared in ZnO-g-C3N4, which was mainly caused by ZnO doping (Kong et al., 2017).

FT-IR was utilized to identify the functional groups on the surface of the resulting materials (Fig. 2B). The pristine g-C3N4 exhibited some distinct peaks, which were corresponded to the breathing mode of the tri-s-triazine units (810 cm−1), the typical stretching vibration modes of aromatic CN heterocycles (1240, 1320, 1410, 1640 cm−1), and the amino groups (broad bands in the region of 3100–3300 cm−1) (Patnaik et al., 2021). The FT-IR spectra of Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4 were similar to that of the pristine g-C3N4. This similarity suggested that the essential surface functional groups of g-C3N4 remained largely unaffected by the incorporation of metal ions or CDs.

The adsorption of MB onto pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4, CDs-g-C3N4 was not obvious (Fig. 2C). To ensure the adsorption-desorption equilibrium has been reached, photocatalysts were dispersed in MB solutions for at least 1 h in darkness before the photocatalytic reaction was initiated. As shown in Fig. 2D, the MB degradation percentages achieved after 6 h of light exposure were 82.8 %, 92.0 %, 91.4 %, 88.6 % and 100 % for pristine g-C3N4, Fe-g-C3N4, ZnO-g-C3N4, Mn-g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4, respectively. This result demonstrated that the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 was enhanced through either metal doping or the incorporation of CDs. The degradation kinetic constant of CDs-g-C3N4 was 0.991 h−1, which was remarkably higher those of pristine g-C3N4 (0.245 h−1), Fe-g-C3N4 (0.300 h−1), ZnO-g-C3N4 (0.339 h−1) and Mn-g-C3N4 (0.288 h−1) (Fig. 2E-F). All of these results proved that CDs modified g-C3N4 had the highest photocatalytic activity. CDs own a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces. This nature enables CDs to effectively combine with substrate materials, leading to the enhancement of substrate materials' surface area and the promotion of electron transfer (Yao et al., 2018; Zhang and Yang, 2024). Therefore, the phenomenon reported here was possibly due to the fact that CDs provided more active sites, and the resultant CDs-g-C3N4 heterojunction structure was more favorable for the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Yuan et al., 2023). To further verify the successful loading of CDs, a TEM was employed to observe the morphologies of pristine g-C3N4 and CDs-g-C3N4. TEM image of CDs-g-C3N4 clearly demonstrated that CDs with diameters of 3–4 nm were uniformly anchored on the g-C3N4 sheets (Fig. S2B). HRTEM images of CDs-g-C3N4 showed that the interplanar spacings of CDs and g-C3N4 were 0.21 nm and 0.33 nm, which were corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively (Fig. S2CD) (Shi et al., 2021). These results verified the successful synthesis of CDs-g-C3N4. Therefore, we chose CDs-g-C3N4 to establish the PMH system. We further investigated the MB degradation percentages caused by different concentrations of CDs-g-C3N4 (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 g/L). As shown in Fig. 3A, the degradation percentage increased significantly when the concentration of CDs-g-C3N4 increased from 0.2 g/L to 1.0 g/L (MB degradation percentage increased from 59.3 % to ~97 % at 3 h). However, when the concentration of CDs-g-C3N4 exceeded 1.0 g/L, the increase of MB degradation percentage was not significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and optimization of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of constructing and utilizing the CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system for lipid synthesis, we explored the effects of light, CDs-g-C3N4 and TEOA (sacrificial electron donors) on the growth of ZZ-46 cells. As shown in Fig. 3B-C, the presence of 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4, 2 g/L TEOA and light illumination at an intensity of 32.8 mW/cm2, did not significantly alter the growth curve of ZZ-46 cells in comparison to the control. After being continuously operated for 120 h, the morphologies of ZZ-46 cells and CDs-g-C3N4 in the PMH system were observed. As demonstrated in Fig. 3D-F, the morphology of CDs-g-C3N4 did not change significantly, and ZZ-46 cells were intact. This result validated that introducing CDs-g-C3N4 into the system did not compromise the structural integrity or viability of ZZ-46 cells. Moreover, this result supported the practicality of employing the CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system for the purpose of lipid synthesis.

We optimized the concentrations of CDs-g-C3N4 and TEOA to maximize lipid production in the PMH system. As shown in Fig. 4A, when the concentration of CDs-g-C3N4 increased from 0 g/L to 1 g/L, the lipid yield of the PMH system increased and reached the highest (1.43 g/L) at 1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4. It is believed that, in the CDs doped g-C3N4 composites, CDs act as the photosensitizers to harvest more visible light (Fang et al., 2016). Since CDs own unique electronic storage and electron transfer properties, the micro-regional heterostructures formed by CDs and g-C3N4 can increase the photocatalyst's specific surface area and promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons (Li et al., 2021). Therefore, the result reported here may be due to the fact that more photogenerated electrons are transferred from the conductive band (CB) of CDs-g-C3N4 to the adjacent ZZ-46 cells, promoting the generation of NADPH and the following lipid synthesis. However, when the concentration of CDs-g-C3N4 exceeded 1 g/L, the lipid yield of the PMH system began to decrease. The possible reason may be that too high concentration of CDs-g-C3N4 produced excessive ROS and inhibited the microbial activity. Similarly, the sacrificial electron donors-TEOA, demonstrated a significant impact on lipid production. A TEOA concentration of 2 g/L emerged as optimal, achieving the highest lipid yield (Fig. 4B). These findings pointed to 1 g/L of CDs-g-C3N4 and 2 g/L of TEOA as the ideal concentrations for harnessing the PMH system's full potential for lipid synthesis. Under this condition, the lipid yield of PMH system was 1.70 g/L at 120 h, which was 36 % higher than that of the control group (Fig. 4C-D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lipid synthesis mechanism of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system
ce:section.ce:para: Lipid synthesis in oleaginous microorganisms requires a large amount of reducing power. In order to unveil the lipid synthesis mechanism of the prepared PMH system, we investigated the effect of CDs-g-C3N4 on the regeneration of NADH in vitro. As shown in Fig. 5A, NADH content was very low under the conditions of either illumination alone or in conjunction with CDs-g-C3N4, indicating that these conditions did not facilitate NADH regeneration. Conversely, a substantial increase in NADH concentration was observed when the system was subjected to light in the presence of both CDs-g-C3N4 and TEOA, indicating that sacrificial electron donors played a pivotal role in this context. In the range of 1–6 h, the concentration of NADH gradually increased with the extension of time (Fig. 5B). These results indicated that photogenerated electrons on the surface of CDs-g-C3N4 can effectively promote NADH regeneration in the presence of sacrificial electron donor, TEOA. We further evaluated the effect of photogenerated electrons on the NADPH/NADP+ ratio in ZZ-46 cells. Under the condition of illumination+1 g/L CDs-g-C3N4 + 2 g/L TEOA, the NADPH/NADP+ ratio in ZZ-46 cells was 1.36, which was significantly higher than those of the other experimental groups (Fig. 5C). These results indicated that in the presence of an appropriate amount of sacrificial electron donor, photogenerated electrons on the surface of CDs-g-C3N4 effectively promoted NADPH regeneration, which was beneficial for lipid synthesis.

Under illumination, when CDs-g-C3N4 was combined with ZZ-46 cells in the PMH system, the intracellular ROS level was higher than those of the other groups, which may be caused by the exogenous ROS produced in the photocatalytic reactions. Interestingly, when TEOA was added, the ROS level in ZZ-46 cells decreased. The possible reason was that TEOA eradicated some photogenerated holes (h+) on the surface of CDs-g-C3N4, which reduced the amount of ROS induced by the photocatalytic reactions (Fig. 5D). Excessive ROS will cause oxidative damage, interfere the intracellular homeostasis and show toxic effects to cells. However, a moderate amount of ROS stimulates cells to activate their homeostasis mechanisms to synthesize more reducing substances (including lipids) to maintain the intracellular redox balance (Zhao et al., 2022). For instance, ROS stimulates the lipid accumulation in chlorella by altering the intracellular metabolic pathways (Wang et al., 2016). Therefore, we hypothesized that a moderate amount of ROS produced in the photocatalytic reactions stimulated oleaginous yeasts to synthesize more lipids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers have tried various methods to treat fermentation residues, especially those containing harmful substances such as antibiotics (Yang et al., 2019; Zhong et al., 2014). Studies have shown that biochar prepared from microbial residue has a high adsorption capacity and great application potentials in environmental remediation(Ge et al., 2020). While the applications of PMH systems in chemical synthesis have been extensively reported, the literatures present very limited information on the management of fermentation residues resulting from such systems. Unlike the traditional fermentation system, the final residue of PMH system contained the photocatalysts, which may cause unexpected environmental risks. In order to treat and reuse the residual biomass of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system after lipid extraction, we collected and freeze-dry the residues, after which the residues were pyrolyzed into biochar (Fig. 6A). As shown in Fig. 6B, normally cultured ZZ-46 cells were mainly spherical. After ultrasonic crushing and freeze-drying, ZZ-46 cell fragments and CDs-g-C3N4 were mixed together, exhibiting an irregular morphology (Fig. 6C). After being treated at 550 °C, the residues of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system was pyrolyzed into biochar, which had porous structures with large specific surface areas (Fig. 6D-E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity evaluation of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar had abundant pores, suggesting its potential as an effective adsorption material. Therefore, we investigated the adsorption of MB onto the derived biochar. Three kinds of biochar (BC-350, BC-450, BC-550) were prepared by pyrolysis at three different temperatures (350 °C,450 °C and 550 °C). We found that these biochar samples had different adsorption capacities for MB (Fig. 7A). After a 1 h adsorption period, BC-550 demonstrated an impressive adsorption rate of 57 %, surpassing the rates of BC-350 (14 %) and BC-450 (38 %). This outcome suggested that the pyrolysis temperature directly impacted the adsorption ability of biochar. The higher pyrolysis temperature likely leads to the increased volatilization of organic substances, thereby enhancing the porosity of the resultant biochar (Ge et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2016), and consequently improving its adsorption properties.

Temperature is an important factor affecting the adsorption properties of biochar (Choi et al., 2020). The adsorption properties of BC-550 at different temperatures (25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7C, the adsorption properties of BC-550 remained relatively stable within the range of 25–45 °C. We further investigated the effect of pH value on the adsorption properties of biochar. When the pH value was in the range of 3–6, the adsorption performance of BC-550 did not exhibit significant changes (Fig. 7D). However, a notable enhancement in the adsorption performance of BC-550 was observed when the pH value exceeded 6. This could be attributed to the cationic nature of MB. Under lower pH conditions, the increased presence of hydrogen ions in the solution results in a positively charged surface of BC-550, thereby limiting its ability to adsorb MB molecules. Conversely, at a pH above 6, the surface of BC-550 becomes negatively charged, facilitating electrostatic attraction and promoting the adsorption of MB molecules onto BC-550.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic dynamics and reusability of CDs-g-C3N4-ZZ-46 PMH system residual biomass derived biochar
ce:section.ce:para: Pseudo-first order and Pseudo-second order kinetic models were applied to evaluate the mechanism and rate-limiting step of MB adsorption onto the BC-550. As shown in Fig. 8A, the MB adsorption behavior on BC-550 fit better with the Pseudo-second order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9965). This result suggested the MB adsorption behavior on BC-550 was mainly chemical adsorption (ion exchange). Reusability is an important parameter to evaluate the quality of an adsorbent. We used ethanol to regenerate BC-550. Interestingly, even after undergoing five cycles of reuse and regeneration, BC-550 consistently demonstrated high adsorption performance, underscoring its excellent reusability (Fig. 8B). More efforts are needed to examine the adsorption of other organic contaminants onto BC-550 for practical applications in the future.

In this work, we synthesized CDs-g-C3N4, which exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity. Subsequently, we established a PMH system utilizing CDs-g-C3N4 in conjunction with C. dermatis ZZ-46 to facilitate lipid production under illumination conditions. The lipid yield of this PMH system reached 1.70 g/L at 120 h, which was greatly higher than the control. The residual biomass of this PMH system after lipid extraction was repurposed to generate biochar through pyrolysis at 550 °C. The resultant biochar, BC-550, displayed exceptional adsorption capabilities and reusability. These outcomes provide valuable insights into addressing energy shortage and environment contamination.
Yadong Yu: Conceptualization. Shanshan Wang: Investigation. Shaopeng Lv: Investigation. Laiyou Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Shuxian Guo: Resources, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the authors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22378196), Open-end Funds of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment (SH20231208), Open Project of Henan Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbial Resources and Fermentation Technology, Nanyang Institute of Technology (HIMFT20210206), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province and Talent research start-up fund of Nanjing Tech University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.171639.
